title: Whittled sycamore needle case
subtitle: Small two-piece cylindrical needle case in green sycamore
excerpt: A two-piece needle case in green sycamore: a 7 cm body and a push-fit cap. The fit of the cap to the body is the main challenge. Sycamore carves cleanly to a round cross-section from any direction.
sourceNotes: Cassell's Cyclopaedia of Mechanics vol. III on small carved treen fittings. Newey and Drage Practical Carpentry on shaped small wooden objects.

glossaryTerms.definition: Freshly felled or recently riven timber, still high in moisture. Carves more easily than seasoned wood under hand tools.
A short length of round or split timber, ready to be worked into a blank for carving.
The safe default cut: blade travelling away from the body, driven by the forearm, off the end of the workpiece into open air.
A short straight-bladed knife with a stout handle; the standard for whittling and small treen work.

content.text: A needle case is two short cylinders: a body that holds the needles and a push-fit cap. The body is 7 cm long; the cap is 2 cm long. Both start as short
billets
of
green

sycamore. Sycamore carves cleanly on the round cross-section from any direction. The fit of the cap is the defining challenge: too loose and the needles fall out; too tight and the cap cannot be removed without tools.
Grip: four-finger grip on the work; thumb-push grip on the knife. Never make a cut whose blade-path can reach a body part.
Cut direction: push cuts and thumb-pivot cuts are the default. Pull cuts only with the work braced against the chest and the blade-arc unable to reach anything vital.
Sharpening: a dull tool slips. Strop every fifteen minutes of carving, hone weekly, oilstone monthly.
First aid: keep a styptic pencil, gauze, and a clean dressing within reach. A cut that shows fat or will not close with pressure goes to A&E, not a plaster.
Workspace: stable seat, good light, no children or pets within arm-and-blade reach.
The wood

Two pieces of straight-grained green sycamore: one 8 cm long and 2.5 cm in diameter for the body; one 3 cm long and 2.5 cm in diameter for the cap. The grain must run along the length of each piece or the walls will be cross-grained and prone to splitting.
Tools
A
sloyd knife
for shaping; a brace and bit (10 mm bit for the body bore, 12 mm for the cap bore); a leather strop; raw linseed oil.
Method
Shape each billet to a round cross-section with
push cuts
. Work around the perimeter in overlapping passes. Check by rolling on the bench. Target diameter: 18 mm.
Bore the body: clamp it end-grain-up in a vise. Use the 10 mm bit, bore 5.5 cm deep into one end. The bore does not go through; it leaves a 1 cm floor at the closed end.
Shape the cap piece to 20 mm external diameter. The cap will push-fit over the top 1.5 cm of the body.
Bore the cap with the 12 mm bit, 1.8 cm deep. Test the fit frequently during boring: the cap should push on with light thumb pressure and stay on without rattling.
If the cap is too tight, thin the body top with light push cuts around the circumference. If the cap is too loose, glue a thin strip of sycamore veneer inside the cap bore to reduce its diameter.
Leave both pieces on a shelf for 2 weeks before oiling. Sycamore shrinks slightly as it dries and the fit may tighten.
Finishing
Sand the exterior of both pieces through 180 and 240 grit. Apply raw linseed oil to the exterior only. Do not oil the bore or the cap interior.
Variations
Longer body for embroidery needles: 10 cm body, same cap dimensions. Bore to 6 mm for long tapestry needles.
Chip-carved ring around the body: a simple V-groove ring at mid-height adds decoration without complicating the fit.
Ash instead of sycamore: similar grain, slightly coarser, equally food-safe.
Care
Wipe down if the case gets wet. Re-oil once a year if in daily use.

items.symptom: Cap splits at the wall during boring
items.cause: Cap wall too thin before boring began
items.fix: Keep the cap external diameter at 22 mm minimum when boring a 12 mm hole. The wall must be at least 3 mm thick after boring.

items.symptom: Cap fits on the bench but becomes loose after drying
items.cause: Body shrank more than the cap as the sycamore dried
items.fix: Glue a thin shim of sycamore veneer inside the cap. One layer of veneer reduces the bore by about 0.8 mm.

items.cause: Brace and bit angled during boring
items.fix: Sight along the brace from two directions before starting. A try-square held beside the brace shows any lean.
